medical.title: Immune biomarkers may predict response to bladder cancer treatment
medical.summary: A Northwestern Medicine study has offered new clues as to why immunotherapy works well for some bladder cancer patients but fails for others, according to a study published in the Journal of Clinical Investigation.

medical.title: Brain gives up secrets in research targeting mental illness
medical.summary: Psychiatrists have long treated depression using transcranial magnetic stimulation—noninvasive magnetic pulses that stimulate neurons. Now, new research is allowing them to fine-tune their approach, potentially targeting specific symptoms and opening new possibilities for precision care.

medical.title: When therapists can't take off the 'hat'
medical.summary: Licensed professional counselors have reported experiencing burnout and emotional exhaustion due to high workplace demands, a problem that has intensified over the past six years. On top of workplace pressures, mental health professionals also sometimes report feelings of confusion and conflict when separating their role as therapists from their interpersonal relationships, leading them to feel even more burned out.

medical.title: CRISPR enzyme precisely detects and shreds DNA in cancer mutations once considered 'undruggable'
medical.summary: In 2020, Jennifer Doudna won the Nobel Prize in chemistry for her work on the CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing technology that allows scientists to precisely modify DNA by cutting it at specific locations. Six years later, a new study in Nature by a team led by Doudna has uncovered a powerful new approach to selectively kill cancer cells using a CRISPR enzyme called Cas12a2.

medical.title: Is milk good or bad for kids? And how much dairy do they actually need?
medical.summary: If you follow child nutrition content on social media, you're bound to be confused when it comes to giving your kids milk. Some influencers claim you should avoid milk at all costs, for fear it could cause asthma, allergies or digestive problems. Others say your child probably isn't drinking enough.

medical.title: Does the body really 'keep the score' after trauma? How the debunked idea of 'repressed memories' is making a comeback
medical.summary: Have you heard someone say online or in casual conversation, when responding to someone's struggles, "well, the body keeps the score"?

medical.title: Biological aging clocks offer insights, not diagnostics
medical.summary: The Journal of Medical Internet Research released a feature story on the accuracy and utility of consumer wearables that estimate so-called biological age in its News and Perspectives section. In "Sorting Science From Marketing in the Era of Data-Driven Biological Aging Clocks," JMIR Correspondent Jenna Congdon breaks down the gimmick—how biosensor-enabled devices use proxy data to approximate age, the differences between consumer models and research-grade clinical clocks, and how users can best interpret these metrics.

medical.title: Oral therapy enables at-home treatment for acute myeloid leukemia
medical.summary: For years, treatment of older patients with acute myeloid leukemia—an aggressive cancer of the blood and bone marrow—has required injections administered in a clinic for five to seven consecutive days each month, in addition to an oral pill. For these older patients, that clinic cadence can be quite intensive.

medical.title: 'Lime bike leg': What doctors are seeing in ebike crashes
medical.summary: A man in his 30s arrived at the Royal London Major Trauma Centre after what sounded like a relatively minor cycling accident. He had been riding a shared ebike when he lost control and fell. By the time I met him, scans had revealed a complex fracture around his ankle where his shinbone (tibia) had pierced his skin. He would need multiple surgeries to fix his broken bone and grafts for his skin and muscles, followed by many months of rehabilitation and a prolonged period away from work.

medical.title: First report on the clinical efficacy of chemoimmunotherapy for ultra-rare urachal cancer
medical.summary: Physician-scientists at Kanazawa University have demonstrated, for the first time, the clinical efficacy of chemoimmunotherapy in patients with ultra-rare urachal cancer. Furthermore, their reverse translational research elucidated the mechanism by which the addition of immunotherapy enhances the efficacy of chemotherapy.

medical.title: Rural drivers at greater risk for impaired driving
medical.summary: Drivers involved in serious accidents in rural areas have a higher prevalence of drunk driving, detectable cannabis and polysubstance use than urban drivers, according to a Canada-wide study published in the journal Traffic Injury Prevention.

medical.title: Canadian women have to advocate for themselves when seeking treatment for high blood pressure: study
medical.summary: Canadian women have to advocate for themselves when it comes to seeking treatment for high blood pressure, according to a new study from a team of researchers at the University of Alberta and the University of Ottawa. The findings are published in the journal CJC Open.

medical.title: GLP-1s may alleviate depression through the microbiome, mouse study suggests
medical.summary: Some people taking GLP-1 drugs for diabetes and obesity experience mental health benefits—particularly a decrease in symptoms of depression. In a mouse model study published in Cell Host & Microbe, researchers report that these mental health improvements appear to result from changes in the gut microbiome that lead to an abundance of a microbial strain known to have a favorable effect on neurons related to stress.

medical.title: Safety concerns biggest barrier to vaccination for parents
medical.summary: Most vaccine-hesitant parents aren't opposed to vaccination but simply desire to protect their children from all harms, according to a review.

medical.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine passes first human trial
medical.summary: Scientists have successfully tested an AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine in humans for the first time, finding it to be safe and well tolerated. The vaccine generated immune responses against multiple coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS, and related bat viruses with pandemic potential. By targeting features shared across an entire virus family, it aims to provide protection even as viruses evolve.

medical.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
medical.summary: Scientists have identified genetic variants that may make some people less responsive to GLP-1 drugs used to treat Type 2 diabetes. Roughly 10% of the population carries these variants, which appear to cause a mysterious form of "GLP-1 resistance." In several clinical trials, carriers were significantly less likely to reach healthy blood sugar targets while taking GLP-1 medications.

medical.title: The biggest collagen study yet reveals what actually works
medical.summary: A major review of nearly 8,000 participants found that collagen supplements can improve skin health and ease osteoarthritis symptoms, especially when taken consistently over longer periods. Researchers also found modest benefits for muscle and tendon health. But the results challenge claims that collagen enhances sports performance, as it showed little effect on recovery or post-workout soreness.

medical.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
medical.summary: Melanoma may not become steadily more dangerous with age as scientists once assumed. In a surprising discovery, researchers found that cancer spread was lowest in young mice, surged in middle-aged mice, and then dropped again in very old mice. The key appears to be a special type of immune cell that helps keep cancer dormant and prevents it from spreading.

medical.title: Scientists say they’ve reversed brain aging with a simple nasal spray
medical.summary: Researchers at Texas A&M have developed a nasal spray that appears to reverse brain aging by calming inflammation and restoring the brain’s energy systems. After just two doses, memory and cognitive function improved for months, raising hopes for future treatments targeting dementia and brain fog.

science.title: Secret life of adult whitebait revealed by new research
science.summary: Te Whare Wānanga o Waitaha | University of Canterbury (UC) School of Biological Sciences researcher Dr. Ben Crichton has studied kōkopu—whitebait species that can live for 10 years or more. Whitebait is the collective term for the juveniles of six species of freshwater fish. Nocturnal, territorial and competitive, the three kōkopu species have received less research attention than īnanga, which make up most of the whitebait catch.

science.title: Human evolution was messy and gradual, not an abrupt revolution, argues archaeologist
science.summary: It is generally accepted by archaeologists that modern humans originated in Africa and dispersed worldwide, while other hominins went extinct. Yet how and when Homo sapiens dispersed out of Africa, and whether it was an abrupt event, is still debated. Even more uncertain is how and when humans went from being "archaic" to "modern."

science.title: Where not to look in the search for ET
science.summary: There's a question at the heart of SETI that doesn't get nearly enough attention. It isn't whether aliens exist, and it isn't whether we have the technology to detect them. It's a far more practical problem: With a billion stars in our galaxy and finite telescope time, how do you decide which ones to actually listen to?

science.title: A meteorite impact may have once rained gold on Western Australia
science.summary: We're used to a lot of different natural things falling out of the sky. These can include snow, rain and sometimes even frogs (yes, really). All of these relate to weather phenomena.

science.title: An underground detector in China unveils its first major findings about mysterious ghost particles
science.summary: A massive underground detector aimed at understanding the mysterious ghost particles in our universe released its first major results on Wednesday.

science.title: Do mandatory body cameras actually reduce police brutality?
science.summary: In early June, an ABC Four Corners investigation into NSW Police showcased firsthand accounts of police using excessive force against the public.

science.title: NASA Equips Astronauts, Industry with Robotic Intelligence
science.summary: As NASA plans long-term missions on the Moon, the agency could use robots to perform routine tasks, allowing crew members to dedicate more time to science and exploration. However, robotic motion control requires complex technology and advances in features like robotic decision-making and object recognition. These are the challenges a Boulder, Colorado-based robotics company is […]

science.title: What happens when cartoon villains have an accent? Research reveals impact on kids
science.summary: When kids watch cartoons, they're absorbing much more than a plot. Thanks to the use of foreign accents in shows, they're also learning a shorthand for moral character, new research from the University of Toronto Mississauga suggests.

science.title: One storm pushed world's rarest great ape closer to extinction in Sumatra
science.summary: Climate change-fueled landslides wiped out nearly one in 10 remaining members of the world's rarest great ape species on Indonesia's Sumatra island, scientists said Wednesday.

science.title: 'Basketball Mathematics' help children boost math skills without extra class time
science.summary: A dribble and a jump shot, followed by a fractions task. That is what physical education classes looked like for a group of pupils, and the pupils not only found the lessons more engaging than usual—they also became better at mathematics with a basketball in their hands, according to a new study from the University of Copenhagen.

science.title: 80-atom boron 'buckyball' finally steps into nanotechnology's spotlight
science.summary: The nanoscale world appears to have a new ball to kick around. Researchers from Brown University have shown the first experimental evidence for a "buckyball" molecule made from 80 boron atoms. The new structure is the cousin of the carbon buckyball, known formally as Buckminsterfullerene—a soccer ball-shaped molecule made from 60 carbon atoms that helped launch the nanotechnology revolution when it was discovered just over 40 years ago.

science.title: Greater international cooperation is needed to achieve the UN's global forest goals
science.summary: While many people tacitly appreciate the intrinsic and aesthetic value forests and trees provide, governments and policymakers have struggled to take firm action to protect Earth's forests.

science.title: Train Ride to NASA Kennedy for Artemis III Booster Segments
science.summary: The final booster motor segments for NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket that will help propel Artemis III astronauts on their journey to space shipped from Northrop Grumman’s Railyard Shipping Facility in Corinne, Utah on June 2. The eight booster motor segments are on their way to NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida where they […]

science.title: A day at the museum: How to follow kids' leads to support curiosity across generations
science.summary: As the school year winds down and families start planning summer activities, possibly anticipating those inevitable rainy days, I have found myself returning to a simple but powerful joy: sharing museum experiences with young children.

science.title: Acoustic environment may explain why some bird songs outlast others
science.summary: From melodic morning choruses to territorial songs that echo through forests and grasslands, birds rely on vocalizations to communicate, attract mates and defend valuable habitat. For songbirds, these vocal displays are not simply inherited instincts. Like human language, many bird songs are learned through a complex process of listening, imitation and practice.

science.title: Giant kelp's microscopic light antenna could inspire innovative climate solutions
science.summary: New research reveals the microscopic machinery that helps giant kelp turn sunlight into energy, providing inspiration for innovative climate solutions. The study, published in Nature Communications, mapped one of the tiny antenna-like structures kelp uses for photosynthesis. Their unique architecture may hold the key to why kelp are the most efficient ocean organisms at capturing light and removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

science.title: When men take parental leave, their careers may benefit—but women's do not
science.summary: Parental leave policies in Canada are designed for both parents, but fathers use them at roughly half the rate of mothers. From 2012 to 2017, Statistics Canada found 88% of mothers took maternity leave, parental leave or a combination of the two, compared with 46% of fathers.

science.title: Colonial ties may reshape 2026 World Cup odds, 1,500 simulations suggest
science.summary: Colonial legacies continue to shape competitive outcomes in international football. A research team led by the University of Zurich has used about 1,500 simulations of the upcoming World Cup to show that former colonial powers benefit from access to a broader pool of talent, while formerly colonized nations face lower chances of success.

science.title: Ancient genome duplications laid the foundations of complex brains, research suggests
science.summary: New findings, published in Nature, help answer the riddle of how vertebrates evolved the diverse array of brain cells that distinguishes them from other animals. It appears that a dramatic expansion of the genetic toolkit more than 450 million years ago enabled the emergence of different kinds of brain cells. These cellular innovations are shared across vertebrates—from primitive fish to mammals—and form the basis of the sophisticated brains seen today.

science.title: How Argonaute, a key protein for RNA therapeutics, becomes activated
science.summary: RNA therapeutics have emerged as one of the most promising new classes of medicines. Eight small interfering RNA (siRNA) drugs have already been approved worldwide for the treatment of genetic diseases, yet scientists have not fully understood one of the most fundamental steps underlying their function: how Argonaute, the core protein responsible for gene silencing, becomes activated.

science.title: Tyndall's Trail of Bergs: Ice splintered off southern Patagonia glacier drifts across a growing glacial lake
science.summary: The Southern Patagonian Icefield is the largest expanse of ice in the Southern Hemisphere outside Antarctica. The mass of glacial ice extends hundreds of kilometers along the spine of the Andes, feeding dozens of dynamic outlet glaciers that grind their way down from higher elevations. Many of these rivers of ice terminate in the sea or in proglacial lakes.

science.title: Retreating glaciers increase iceberg sightings and reshape deep-sea habitats
science.summary: The number of icebergs in the Arctic has increased sharply since the 2000s. This is due to the destabilization of large glaciers in northeast Greenland and parts of the Russian Arctic, as well as the increasing mobility of sea ice. The result: Stones rain down from the melting icebergs, forming new hard-substrate habitats for marine life on the soft seafloor.

science.title: Newfound 'whale necropolis' reveals 5.3 million years of seafloor life
science.summary: Whale falls form when whale carcasses sink to the seafloor, creating localized concentrations of biodiversity in the deep ocean. Besides playing a role in long-term carbon sequestration, whale falls help scientists understand the evolution and dispersal of deep-sea life, as well as provide an unprecedented fossil record of whales and deep-sea ecosystems.

science.title: Deep sea an untapped 'evolutionary engine' as dataset yields 500 million unique genes
science.summary: The deep sea is a unique "evolutionary engine," with one of the richest and most unexplored sources of genetic diversity on Earth, according to a major new study that assessed its potential to transform biotechnology and DNA sequencing technologies.

science.title: Microbial alliances, not mitochondria alone, may have built first eukaryotic cells
science.summary: All cells in animals, plants, fungi, and protists share a fundamental characteristic: they are eukaryotic cells—complex cells with specialized internal compartments. The cells that make up our bodies are no exception.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: Fonduri europene 2026: Granturi IMM de câte 50.000-200.000 EUR pentru firme din București și Ilfov. Descarcă propunerea de ghid pentru finanțarea „proof of concept”

mix_items.title: Secret life of adult whitebait revealed by new research
mix_items.summary: Te Whare Wānanga o Waitaha | University of Canterbury (UC) School of Biological Sciences researcher Dr. Ben Crichton has studied kōkopu—whitebait species that can live for 10 years or more. Whitebait is the collective term for the juveniles of six species of freshwater fish. Nocturnal, territorial and competitive, the three kōkopu species have received less research attention than īnanga, which make up most of the whitebait catch.

mix_items.title: Brain gives up secrets in research targeting mental illness
mix_items.summary: Psychiatrists have long treated depression using transcranial magnetic stimulation—noninvasive magnetic pulses that stimulate neurons. Now, new research is allowing them to fine-tune their approach, potentially targeting specific symptoms and opening new possibilities for precision care.

mix_items.title: Human evolution was messy and gradual, not an abrupt revolution, argues archaeologist
mix_items.summary: It is generally accepted by archaeologists that modern humans originated in Africa and dispersed worldwide, while other hominins went extinct. Yet how and when Homo sapiens dispersed out of Africa, and whether it was an abrupt event, is still debated. Even more uncertain is how and when humans went from being "archaic" to "modern."

mix_items.title: Where not to look in the search for ET
mix_items.summary: There's a question at the heart of SETI that doesn't get nearly enough attention. It isn't whether aliens exist, and it isn't whether we have the technology to detect them. It's a far more practical problem: With a billion stars in our galaxy and finite telescope time, how do you decide which ones to actually listen to?

mix_items.title: When therapists can't take off the 'hat'
mix_items.summary: Licensed professional counselors have reported experiencing burnout and emotional exhaustion due to high workplace demands, a problem that has intensified over the past six years. On top of workplace pressures, mental health professionals also sometimes report feelings of confusion and conflict when separating their role as therapists from their interpersonal relationships, leading them to feel even more burned out.

mix_items.title: A meteorite impact may have once rained gold on Western Australia
mix_items.summary: We're used to a lot of different natural things falling out of the sky. These can include snow, rain and sometimes even frogs (yes, really). All of these relate to weather phenomena.

mix_items.title: CRISPR enzyme precisely detects and shreds DNA in cancer mutations once considered 'undruggable'
mix_items.summary: In 2020, Jennifer Doudna won the Nobel Prize in chemistry for her work on the CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing technology that allows scientists to precisely modify DNA by cutting it at specific locations. Six years later, a new study in Nature by a team led by Doudna has uncovered a powerful new approach to selectively kill cancer cells using a CRISPR enzyme called Cas12a2.

mix_items.title: An underground detector in China unveils its first major findings about mysterious ghost particles
mix_items.summary: A massive underground detector aimed at understanding the mysterious ghost particles in our universe released its first major results on Wednesday.

mix_items.title: Do mandatory body cameras actually reduce police brutality?
mix_items.summary: In early June, an ABC Four Corners investigation into NSW Police showcased firsthand accounts of police using excessive force against the public.

mix_items.title: Is milk good or bad for kids? And how much dairy do they actually need?
mix_items.summary: If you follow child nutrition content on social media, you're bound to be confused when it comes to giving your kids milk. Some influencers claim you should avoid milk at all costs, for fear it could cause asthma, allergies or digestive problems. Others say your child probably isn't drinking enough.

However, robotic motion control requires complex technology and advances in features like robotic decision-making and object recognition. mix_items.title: What happens when cartoon villains have an accent? Research reveals impact on kids
mix_items.summary: When kids watch cartoons, they're absorbing much more than a plot. Thanks to the use of foreign accents in shows, they're also learning a shorthand for moral character, new research from the University of Toronto Mississauga suggests.

mix_items.title: One storm pushed world's rarest great ape closer to extinction in Sumatra
mix_items.summary: Climate change-fueled landslides wiped out nearly one in 10 remaining members of the world's rarest great ape species on Indonesia's Sumatra island, scientists said Wednesday.

mix_items.title: 'Basketball Mathematics' help children boost math skills without extra class time
mix_items.summary: A dribble and a jump shot, followed by a fractions task. That is what physical education classes looked like for a group of pupils, and the pupils not only found the lessons more engaging than usual—they also became better at mathematics with a basketball in their hands, according to a new study from the University of Copenhagen.

mix_items.title: Does the body really 'keep the score' after trauma? How the debunked idea of 'repressed memories' is making a comeback
mix_items.summary: Have you heard someone say online or in casual conversation, when responding to someone's struggles, "well, the body keeps the score"?

mix_items.title: Biological aging clocks offer insights, not diagnostics
mix_items.summary: The Journal of Medical Internet Research released a feature story on the accuracy and utility of consumer wearables that estimate so-called biological age in its News and Perspectives section. In "Sorting Science From Marketing in the Era of Data-Driven Biological Aging Clocks," JMIR Correspondent Jenna Congdon breaks down the gimmick—how biosensor-enabled devices use proxy data to approximate age, the differences between consumer models and research-grade clinical clocks, and how users can best interpret these metrics.

mix_items.title: 80-atom boron 'buckyball' finally steps into nanotechnology's spotlight
mix_items.summary: The nanoscale world appears to have a new ball to kick around. Researchers from Brown University have shown the first experimental evidence for a "buckyball" molecule made from 80 boron atoms. The new structure is the cousin of the carbon buckyball, known formally as Buckminsterfullerene—a soccer ball-shaped molecule made from 60 carbon atoms that helped launch the nanotechnology revolution when it was discovered just over 40 years ago.

mix_items.title: Greater international cooperation is needed to achieve the UN's global forest goals
mix_items.summary: While many people tacitly appreciate the intrinsic and aesthetic value forests and trees provide, governments and policymakers have struggled to take firm action to protect Earth's forests.

mix_items.summary: The final booster motor segments for NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket that will help propel Artemis III astronauts on their journey to space shipped from Northrop Grumman’s Railyard Shipping Facility in Corinne, Utah on June 2. The eight booster motor segments are on their way to NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida where they […]

mix_items.title: A day at the museum: How to follow kids' leads to support curiosity across generations
mix_items.summary: As the school year winds down and families start planning summer activities, possibly anticipating those inevitable rainy days, I have found myself returning to a simple but powerful joy: sharing museum experiences with young children.

mix_items.title: Oral therapy enables at-home treatment for acute myeloid leukemia
mix_items.summary: For years, treatment of older patients with acute myeloid leukemia—an aggressive cancer of the blood and bone marrow—has required injections administered in a clinic for five to seven consecutive days each month, in addition to an oral pill. For these older patients, that clinic cadence can be quite intensive.

mix_items.title: 'Lime bike leg': What doctors are seeing in ebike crashes
mix_items.summary: A man in his 30s arrived at the Royal London Major Trauma Centre after what sounded like a relatively minor cycling accident. He had been riding a shared ebike when he lost control and fell. By the time I met him, scans had revealed a complex fracture around his ankle where his shinbone (tibia) had pierced his skin. He would need multiple surgeries to fix his broken bone and grafts for his skin and muscles, followed by many months of rehabilitation and a prolonged period away from work.

mix_items.title: Acoustic environment may explain why some bird songs outlast others
mix_items.summary: From melodic morning choruses to territorial songs that echo through forests and grasslands, birds rely on vocalizations to communicate, attract mates and defend valuable habitat. For songbirds, these vocal displays are not simply inherited instincts. Like human language, many bird songs are learned through a complex process of listening, imitation and practice.

mix_items.title: Giant kelp's microscopic light antenna could inspire innovative climate solutions
mix_items.summary: New research reveals the microscopic machinery that helps giant kelp turn sunlight into energy, providing inspiration for innovative climate solutions. The study, published in Nature Communications, mapped one of the tiny antenna-like structures kelp uses for photosynthesis. Their unique architecture may hold the key to why kelp are the most efficient ocean organisms at capturing light and removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

mix_items.title: First report on the clinical efficacy of chemoimmunotherapy for ultra-rare urachal cancer
mix_items.summary: Physician-scientists at Kanazawa University have demonstrated, for the first time, the clinical efficacy of chemoimmunotherapy in patients with ultra-rare urachal cancer. Furthermore, their reverse translational research elucidated the mechanism by which the addition of immunotherapy enhances the efficacy of chemotherapy.

mix_items.title: When men take parental leave, their careers may benefit—but women's do not
mix_items.summary: Parental leave policies in Canada are designed for both parents, but fathers use them at roughly half the rate of mothers. From 2012 to 2017, Statistics Canada found 88% of mothers took maternity leave, parental leave or a combination of the two, compared with 46% of fathers.

mix_items.title: Colonial ties may reshape 2026 World Cup odds, 1,500 simulations suggest
mix_items.summary: Colonial legacies continue to shape competitive outcomes in international football. A research team led by the University of Zurich has used about 1,500 simulations of the upcoming World Cup to show that former colonial powers benefit from access to a broader pool of talent, while formerly colonized nations face lower chances of success.

mix_items.title: Rural drivers at greater risk for impaired driving
mix_items.summary: Drivers involved in serious accidents in rural areas have a higher prevalence of drunk driving, detectable cannabis and polysubstance use than urban drivers, according to a Canada-wide study published in the journal Traffic Injury Prevention.

mix_items.title: Ancient genome duplications laid the foundations of complex brains, research suggests
mix_items.summary: New findings, published in Nature, help answer the riddle of how vertebrates evolved the diverse array of brain cells that distinguishes them from other animals. It appears that a dramatic expansion of the genetic toolkit more than 450 million years ago enabled the emergence of different kinds of brain cells. These cellular innovations are shared across vertebrates—from primitive fish to mammals—and form the basis of the sophisticated brains seen today.

mix_items.title: How Argonaute, a key protein for RNA therapeutics, becomes activated
mix_items.summary: RNA therapeutics have emerged as one of the most promising new classes of medicines. Eight small interfering RNA (siRNA) drugs have already been approved worldwide for the treatment of genetic diseases, yet scientists have not fully understood one of the most fundamental steps underlying their function: how Argonaute, the core protein responsible for gene silencing, becomes activated.

mix_items.title: Tyndall's Trail of Bergs: Ice splintered off southern Patagonia glacier drifts across a growing glacial lake
mix_items.summary: The Southern Patagonian Icefield is the largest expanse of ice in the Southern Hemisphere outside Antarctica. The mass of glacial ice extends hundreds of kilometers along the spine of the Andes, feeding dozens of dynamic outlet glaciers that grind their way down from higher elevations. Many of these rivers of ice terminate in the sea or in proglacial lakes.

mix_items.title: Retreating glaciers increase iceberg sightings and reshape deep-sea habitats
mix_items.summary: The number of icebergs in the Arctic has increased sharply since the 2000s. This is due to the destabilization of large glaciers in northeast Greenland and parts of the Russian Arctic, as well as the increasing mobility of sea ice. The result: Stones rain down from the melting icebergs, forming new hard-substrate habitats for marine life on the soft seafloor.

mix_items.title: Newfound 'whale necropolis' reveals 5.3 million years of seafloor life
mix_items.summary: Whale falls form when whale carcasses sink to the seafloor, creating localized concentrations of biodiversity in the deep ocean. Besides playing a role in long-term carbon sequestration, whale falls help scientists understand the evolution and dispersal of deep-sea life, as well as provide an unprecedented fossil record of whales and deep-sea ecosystems.

mix_items.title: Deep sea an untapped 'evolutionary engine' as dataset yields 500 million unique genes
mix_items.summary: The deep sea is a unique "evolutionary engine," with one of the richest and most unexplored sources of genetic diversity on Earth, according to a major new study that assessed its potential to transform biotechnology and DNA sequencing technologies.

mix_items.title: Canadian women have to advocate for themselves when seeking treatment for high blood pressure: study
mix_items.summary: Canadian women have to advocate for themselves when it comes to seeking treatment for high blood pressure, according to a new study from a team of researchers at the University of Alberta and the University of Ottawa. The findings are published in the journal CJC Open.

mix_items.title: GLP-1s may alleviate depression through the microbiome, mouse study suggests
mix_items.summary: Some people taking GLP-1 drugs for diabetes and obesity experience mental health benefits—particularly a decrease in symptoms of depression. In a mouse model study published in Cell Host & Microbe, researchers report that these mental health improvements appear to result from changes in the gut microbiome that lead to an abundance of a microbial strain known to have a favorable effect on neurons related to stress.

mix_items.title: Microbial alliances, not mitochondria alone, may have built first eukaryotic cells
mix_items.summary: All cells in animals, plants, fungi, and protists share a fundamental characteristic: they are eukaryotic cells—complex cells with specialized internal compartments. The cells that make up our bodies are no exception.

mix_items.title: Safety concerns biggest barrier to vaccination for parents
mix_items.summary: Most vaccine-hesitant parents aren't opposed to vaccination but simply desire to protect their children from all harms, according to a review.

mix_items.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine passes first human trial
mix_items.summary: Scientists have successfully tested an AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine in humans for the first time, finding it to be safe and well tolerated. The vaccine generated immune responses against multiple coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS, and related bat viruses with pandemic potential. By targeting features shared across an entire virus family, it aims to provide protection even as viruses evolve.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
mix_items.summary: Scientists have identified genetic variants that may make some people less responsive to GLP-1 drugs used to treat Type 2 diabetes. Roughly 10% of the population carries these variants, which appear to cause a mysterious form of "GLP-1 resistance." In several clinical trials, carriers were significantly less likely to reach healthy blood sugar targets while taking GLP-1 medications.

mix_items.title: The biggest collagen study yet reveals what actually works
mix_items.summary: A major review of nearly 8,000 participants found that collagen supplements can improve skin health and ease osteoarthritis symptoms, especially when taken consistently over longer periods. Researchers also found modest benefits for muscle and tendon health. But the results challenge claims that collagen enhances sports performance, as it showed little effect on recovery or post-workout soreness.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
mix_items.summary: Melanoma may not become steadily more dangerous with age as scientists once assumed. In a surprising discovery, researchers found that cancer spread was lowest in young mice, surged in middle-aged mice, and then dropped again in very old mice. The key appears to be a special type of immune cell that helps keep cancer dormant and prevents it from spreading.

mix_items.title: Scientists say they’ve reversed brain aging with a simple nasal spray
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Texas A&M have developed a nasal spray that appears to reverse brain aging by calming inflammation and restoring the brain’s energy systems. After just two doses, memory and cognitive function improved for months, raising hopes for future treatments targeting dementia and brain fog.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Ce ai rezolvat? - Un sendvis si cateva emotii.

satire_ro.title: Nu doar urşii sunt periculoşi! Turist ardelean, atacat fulgerător de o ţestoasă în Munţii Dobrogei
